audience: Engineers integrating SSO into an existing app

mode_descriptions.dev: Local dev: client on localhost:3000, Keycloak on localhost:8081, no HTTPS
mode_descriptions.prod: Public app, IdP behind CDN, HTTPS everywhere, refresh tokens in httpOnly cookies

flows.name: OAuth2 Login (Authorization Code + PKCE)
flows.purpose: Initial user login. Redirects to IdP, comes back with code, exchanges for tokens.
step_outline: client → user : 302 redirect to IdP authorize URL with code_challenge
idp → user    : 302 back to client /callback with ?code=...
client → idp  : POST /token with code + code_verifier

flows.name: Token Refresh
flows.purpose: Access token expired. Use refresh token to get a new one without user interaction.

flows.name: First-time User Provisioning
flows.purpose: After successful OAuth, the user doesn't exist locally. Create their profile from claims in the id_token.

lessons: The IdP is the only node where the 'tech' string is dramatically different between dev and prod (different products entirely: Keycloak vs Auth0). Use mode to swap the label too.
PKCE matters because the SPA can't keep a secret. The code_verifier is generated client-side and only sent on the token exchange — show this in step 1 and step 6.
Refresh flow has no user interaction. If you find yourself drawing a 'user' node in this flow, you've conflated user-the-actor with user-the-browser-tab.
Logout has a backend revoke step that's easy to forget. Without it, the access token works until it expires even after the user 'logged out'.
